skill.name: controlnet-pose
skill.description: Routes pose-conditioned image and video generation requests to RunComfy models through the runcomfy CLI.
skill.summary: Generate images or videos from pose, motion, depth, or canny references using RunComfy routes.
skill.author: agentspace-so

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many shell, network, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns, but review found no prompt injection, malware, or data exfiltration intent in SKILL.md. Most shell and crypto hits are Markdown code formatting, model names, examples, and documentation text; the confirmed risk is intentional use of the networked runcomfy CLI with local token storage.

medium_findings.title: Networked CLI Execution Requires User Trust
medium_findings.description: The skill allows Bash(runcomfy *) and documents runcomfy commands that submit prompts, asset URLs, and generation requests to RunComfy. This is expected behavior for the skill, but it executes an external CLI and sends user-provided creative inputs to a third-party service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command surface and API behavior are explicitly documented in SKILL.md. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the command is scoped to runcomfy and the service endpoints are disclosed.

medium_findings.title: Local Token Storage in User Config Directory
medium_findings.description: The skill states that runcomfy login writes an API token to ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json with mode 0600. This is normal CLI credential storage, but users should understand that the skill depends on a local credential file or RUNCOMFY_TOKEN.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token path and file mode are directly documented. I found no evidence that the skill reads, transmits, or exposes unrelated files.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation, Catalog Links, and Disclosed Service Endpoints
low_findings.description: The network findings point to RunComfy documentation, model catalog links, placeholder user asset URLs, and an outbound endpoint allowlist. These are relevant for transparency but do not show hidden telemetry or unauthorized exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible Markdown references, example CDN placeholders, or a stated allowlist. No evidence found of covert destinations or secret-bearing requests.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks and Text Were Misclassified as Code or Cryptography
low_findings.description: The Ruby backtick and weak cryptography alerts are false positives from Markdown formatting, command examples, model identifiers, reference text, and an exit-code table. No Ruby code, cryptographic implementation, or weak hashing operation is present in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found prose and fenced bash examples, not executable Ruby or crypto code. The file is a single Markdown skill document with an allowed-tools declaration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Networked CLI Execution Requires User Trust
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill allows Bash(runcomfy *) and documents runcomfy commands that submit prompts, asset URLs, and generation requests to RunComfy. This is expected behavior for the skill, but it executes an external CLI and sends user-provided creative inputs to a third-party service.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command surface and API behavior are explicitly documented in SKILL.md. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the command is scoped to runcomfy and the service endpoints are disclosed.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Token Storage in User Config Directory
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill states that runcomfy login writes an API token to ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json with mode 0600. This is normal CLI credential storage, but users should understand that the skill depends on a local credential file or RUNCOMFY_TOKEN.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The token path and file mode are directly documented. I found no evidence that the skill reads, transmits, or exposes unrelated files.

content.value_statement: Pose and motion references are difficult to route to the right generation model. This skill selects RunComfy routes for pose transfer, ControlNet image generation, and related workflows.
actual_capabilities: Routes video pose transfer requests to Kling Motion Control Pro or Standard.
Routes pose-conditioned image requests to Z-Image Turbo ControlNet LoRA.
Explains when to use video, image, depth, canny, or motion references.
Provides runcomfy CLI invocation examples with JSON input and output directories.
Points users to RunComfy ComfyUI workflows for multi-condition ControlNet stacks.
limitations: Requires the runcomfy CLI and a valid RunComfy account or token.
Does not generate OpenPose or DWPose control images by itself.
Single-route CLI examples do not cover complex multi-condition ComfyUI workflows.
External URLs and generated outputs are processed by RunComfy services.

use_cases.title: Transfer choreography to a character
use_cases.description: Use a source performance video and target character image to create a motion-controlled video.

use_cases.title: Create a pose-locked concept image
use_cases.description: Use an OpenPose, DWPose, canny, or depth image to guide the generated composition.

use_cases.title: Choose the correct RunComfy route
use_cases.description: Compare model routes before running a job that needs pose, motion, or depth conditioning.

prompt_templates.title: Basic pose image
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner image generation with one control image.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an image using this pose reference URL and this prompt. Use the RunComfy pose route that fits a still image.

prompt_templates.title: Video motion transfer
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate video generation from a reference performance.
prompt_templates.prompt: Transfer the motion from this reference video to this character image. Choose the appropriate Kling Motion Control route and prepare the runcomfy command.

prompt_templates.title: Route comparison
prompt_templates.scenario: Plan a job before spending generation credits.
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare the RunComfy routes for my pose task. Explain which route fits best, what inputs I need, and what limitations apply.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced control stack planning
prompt_templates.prompt: I need pose, depth, and reference-image conditioning. Identify whether the CLI route is enough or whether I should use a RunComfy ComfyUI workflow.

output_examples.input: A user provides a dance video and a mascot character image.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends Kling Motion Control, explains the required video and image inputs, and prepares a RunComfy run plan.

output_examples.input: A user provides an OpenPose skeleton and asks for a cinematic character still.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects the Z-Image ControlNet LoRA route and describes the prompt, control image, and output folder setup.

output_examples.input: A user asks for pose plus depth plus reference-image conditioning.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains that the documented CLI routes are single-condition and points to RunComfy ComfyUI workflows.

best_practices: Use clean, user-approved reference videos and images with one clear subject.
Host control assets at stable URLs before starting a RunComfy CLI job.
Choose Pro routes for final video work and lower-cost routes for drafts.
anti_patterns: Do not pass third-party URLs that the user did not explicitly approve.
Do not use image pose routes for video motion transfer tasks.
Do not expect the skill to create pose skeletons or depth maps automatically.

faq.question: Does this skill run generation locally?
faq.answer: No. It uses the runcomfy CLI to send jobs to RunComfy services.

faq.question: Can it create OpenPose or DWPose control images?
faq.answer: No. Create those control images with another tool, then provide their URLs.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What inputs are needed for video pose transfer?
faq.answer: Use a reference performance video and a target character image.

faq.question: What inputs are needed for still-image pose control?
faq.answer: Use a text prompt and a control image such as OpenPose, DWPose, canny, or depth.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning because it uses a networked CLI and local token storage.
